title: Woven floor mat on a frame loom
subtitle: A durable plain-weave mat in cotton rag strips and jute on a simple frame loom
excerpt: Weave a sturdy floor mat on a simple frame loom using strips of recycled cotton fabric for the weft and strong jute twine for the warp. The finished mat is about 40 cm x 60 cm and suitable for use in doorways, kitchens, or under a plant pot.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional rag-rug and frame-loom weaving practice. Plain-weave floor mats from recycled fabric are documented in British and Scandinavian textile heritage as a household weaving tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: The threads stretched lengthways on the loom, under tension. In a floor mat, the warp is the structural element; jute or strong cotton twine works well because the warp is invisible in the finished mat.
The thread or strip passed across the width of the loom through each shed. In a rag mat, the weft is wide strips of recycled fabric that cover the warp entirely.

The simplest weave structure: each weft pass goes alternately over and under each warp thread. Used here for the full mat: it produces a very stable, durable surface when the weft strips are packed tightly.

content.text: A frame-loom floor mat is woven in
with wide strips of recycled cotton fabric as the
and a strong jute or cotton twine as the
. The warp is set closely together and beaten down firmly so the warp threads are completely hidden by the fabric strips. Old cotton T-shirts, sheets, and denim all work well as weft material.

items.name: Recycled cotton fabric strips, cut 2 to 3 cm wide
Jute twine or strong cotton twine (warp)
Frame loom at least 45 cm wide and 65 cm long
Wide stick shuttle or bent-wood shuttle

content.text: Wind the jute twine around the frame in continuous warp threads, spacing them about 1 cm apart. For a 40 cm wide mat, this gives about 40 warp threads. The jute must be under firm, even tension; a loosely warped frame produces a floppy mat.

Cut or tear the cotton fabric into strips 2 to 3 cm wide. Fold the raw edges of each strip inward and roll loosely onto the stick shuttle. Mix colours or keep to a single colour depending on the design. Join new strips by overlapping the ends in the same shed.

Use the shed-stick to open alternate sheds, then pass the shuttle through. Beat each weft strip firmly against the previous one using a wide-tooth comb or the flat edge of the shed-stick. The strips must be packed tightly enough that the jute warp is invisible from the face.

When the mat has reached the desired length, remove it from the frame by sliding it off the warp threads. Knot the warp ends together in pairs and tuck the tails under the edge of the mat. A few rows of hand-stitching across each short end will further secure the weft strips.

items.problem: The mat curls at the corners
items.cause: Uneven tension in the warp, or the fabric strips were pulled too tightly through the shed
items.fix: Leave a slight arc in each weft strip before beating rather than pulling the strip taut. Check warp tension at each corner.

items.problem: The jute warp is visible on the face of the mat
items.cause: The weft strips were not beaten tightly enough
items.fix: Beat more firmly and pack each strip as closely as possible to the previous one. Use a slightly wider strip if the problem persists.
